chapters.title: Do not marry until you know what you are choosing
chapters.text:
Marriage cuts both ways. Done well, it becomes a deep well of joy; done poorly, it can feel like shared misery — as serious as Thomas suggests when he compares a bad union to enduring hell together. That stakes the decision high, so partner selection deserves more deliberation than most people give it.

Too many singles enter marriage without a blueprint. They plan careers and finances for decades yet answer "What kind of marriage do you want?" with little more than "a happy home." Press for details and answers thin out. This summary aims to fix that gap.

Infatuation delivers an emotional high, but research suggests that within roughly twenty-four months the brain chemistry settles. Then hidden flaws surface and overlooked problems become visible.

Every infatuated person believes their partner is the best human being in the world until they get off the emotional cliff.

Slow down. Let biochemistry cool before promising forever. Guidance from experienced counsel saves years of regret — which is what follows in these pages. You will gain tools to clarify what to want in a lifelong union, traits that sustain marriage, and reasons the soulmate myth misleads people into believing only one match exists.

Thomas writes for Christians navigating dating, yet the logic applies broadly: marriage is a vow with decades of consequences, not a prize for winning the courtship season.

Don't rush into marriage until you're certain you're making the right choice for your future. Infatuation is data about chemistry, not destiny. Let time reveal whether admiration survives ordinary Tuesdays.

What you're about to read will equip you with the wisdom you need to decide what exactly you should want in a lifelong relationship, show you traits to imbibe for a successful marriage, and reveal why soulmate is a myth, and show you why there's not just one person you can marry.

chapters.title: Rank character above chemistry
chapters.text:
Marriage bundles many factors; several matter, but not equally.

Physical attraction belongs in the equation — you should desire your spouse — yet it cannot head the list. Chemistry may launch a relationship; it cannot carry decades of bills, parenting, illness, and ordinary Tuesday evenings.

Many women follow romantic fantasy; many men follow sexual pull. Before you can make a wise marital choice, you have to rid yourself of inferior motivations. The wrong why will lead you to the wrong who. ~ Gary L. Thomas

Generally, many people prioritize romance and sexual attraction above character when dating. A few years into marriage, they realize integrity matters more than heartbeat acceleration.

Examine your partner's character while emotions still allow honesty:
• Would you choose them as mother or father of your children?
• Based on habits today, what will marriage look like in ten years?
• When stress piles up, will they remain loyal or disappear?

A sexual partner is often a fair-weather friend; a true lover serves unselfishly through difficulty.

Marry someone who loves you, not someone who merely desires you.

Bonus point: selfless partners tend to create lasting sexual satisfaction because intimacy itself is an act of giving.

Did you know? We attract what we embody. Players attract players; generous lovers attract generous lovers — so cultivate the traits you hope to find.

Stop looking out for the less important things: height, hype, and novelty fade. The questions that matter are boring on Instagram and essential in hospice — will this person show up when the story stops being fun?

Although this isn't one of those religious teachings that tell you to completely forget about sexual appeal, it's important to marry someone you're sexually attracted to. But don't settle for a man or woman that you can't imagine seeing their nakedness — sexual attraction mustn't be number one on your priority list.

The reason is simple: sexual chemistry may sustain a relationship, but it's not enough to take you through the lifelong commitment that marriage requires.

chapters.title: The soulmate story is a myth
chapters.text:
Plato popularized the idea of a single other half completing us. His legend described original humans as fused pairs so powerful they threatened the gods. Zeus split them, leaving each half wandering earth searching for reunion — supposedly explaining human longing for union.

As a Christian, you must understand that this isn't true. It's just a myth. The Bible clearly states that God created male and female humans and instituted marriage. However, the Bible does not say you can't be happy as a single man or woman, nor that there's only one human created for you to marry.

The reason for marriage is that two are better than one. That's it, no more, no less. And marriage should be something that helps you pursue God's kingdom, not what pushes you from it.

Being single is not a crime. It's better to be single and pursue God's kingdom than go into a marriage that will ruin your relationship with God.

In 1 Corinthians 7:8–9, Paul admonishes single and divorced Christians to remain unmarried like him, but that they should get married if being single would cause them to fall into sexual sin.

This clearly tells us that marriage is a matter of choice, not something you have to do because you need to be complete.

You're already loved by God. The idea of getting married is that you're seeking someone who would share that love with you. Debunk the mindset that just one person out there is meant for you. When searching, focus on character and spiritual health, not fireworks mistaken for fate.

Plato's split-soul legend is poetic but pastorally dangerous: it trains people to treat compatibility problems as proof they married the wrong half instead of doing the harder work of growth. Scripture offers a sturdier frame — choose wisely, love faithfully, pursue God together.

chapters.title: Finding a spouse requires intentional effort
chapters.text:
Some Christians are so lazy they outsource to God the things they are supposed to do themselves. Later, when they get disappointed, they begin to resent and blame God unnecessarily.

It's not logical to think you can sit in your comfort zone, and God will bring a life partner to you. It doesn't work that way, yet you see a lot of Christians doing it.

There are few things in life that God does for us without our express participation, but finding a life partner isn't one of them.

You need to put yourself in places and positions that make it easy for you to access potential life partners. We've settled the fact that there isn't just one person out there uniquely created for you, so this should be easy. Your campus, workplace, and churches filled with singles are good places to find potential life partners. The more you expose yourself, the more options you'll have.

Note that the place you go to search is important. You aren't likely to find a compatible man or woman in a raunchy bar, so go where you think your ideal match would be found.

According to The Knot Real Wedding Study, the average age for marriage in America as of 2020 is 33 for men and 31 for women. Other studies agree. Yet sexual drives awaken more than a decade earlier — one reason many indulge in premarital sex.

Some keep chaste until marriage; others battle desire. Know yourself. If abstinence is unrealistic, early marriage may beat pretense that collapses into hidden sin.

The problem with pretension is that you can't do it for long. Sooner or later, your real character would show forth — and these things can be messy. Early marriage can tame sexual urges and keep you from jumping from one partner to another.

Financial difficulty is most people's excuse for delaying marriage, yet a job and shared dream can suffice in your twenties. The spiritual, emotional, and physical benefits of one committed partner across life are enormous. You need to actively search — not wait in isolation — while trusting God with outcomes you cannot control.

chapters.title: Align on marriage style before the vows
chapters.text:
Couples routinely skip a conversation that should precede every engagement: what kind of marriage are we actually building? Shared infatuation is not shared vision. Some partners accidentally want the same future; many discover divergence only after the vows.

Love intensifies assumptions. Thomas urges replacing assumptions with explicit comparison of expectations before the wedding, not during the first crisis.

If you are unsure what you prefer, scan these common models — not a complete catalog, but a useful mirror:
• Kingdom-centered partners

Marriage as public witness to God's love, shaping daily habits and parenting choices.
• Builders versus romantics

Some couples bond chiefly around mission — business, ministry, brand, movement. Others center emotional intimacy above all external goals. The two orientations often collide without negotiation.
• Beauty-first unions

Attraction launches the bond and is expected to carry it. Aging bodies frequently expose how thin that foundation is.
• Rule-bound partnerships

Rigid scripts govern dating milestones, family access, and household routines — often fed by endless advice literature and counseling appointments.
• Conflict addicts

Partners cycle through harm and reconciliation, mistaking volatility for passion. Thomas warns this pattern is destructive and should be avoided entirely.

Identify the model that fits you, then ask your partner which picture they carry. Identical answers are uncommon; early honesty about gaps prevents years of silent disappointment.

Discuss marriage styles with your partner before tying the knot. A founder who expects a co-builder will resent a spouse who expected candlelit conversation every evening. A romantic who craves presence will feel abandoned beside a spouse chasing expansion. None of these tensions is fatal if named early; unnamed, they become identity wars.

Thomas is not asking you to manufacture identical dreams. He is asking you to compare blueprints while you still have options. If one partner wants a horror-show drama cycle and you want peace, that is not a difference to negotiate — it is a reason to pause.

Schedule a deliberate conversation — not a distracted car ride — and walk through each model aloud. Listen for enthusiasm, hesitation, and silence. Silence often marks the unspoken expectation that will later feel like betrayal.

chapters.title: Choose a partner for the hard decades, not the honeymoon
chapters.text:
Courtship spotlights the pleasant present; marriage tests the unedited future. Children may arrive. Health may fail. Income may vanish. The question is not whether difficulty appears but whether your partner leans in when it does.

Research suggests young adults underestimate life's hardness by half. Romance rarely prepares couples for chronic stress, yet that is where vows matter most.

Run uncomfortable scenarios before the altar:
• Two years of illness requiring daily care — does your partner stay or withdraw?
• Parenting a child with special needs — do they serve or resent?
• Long unemployment — do they remain faithful or seek provision elsewhere?

Fair-weather companions shine on vacation and disappear in hospital rooms. Choose someone whose loyalty is already visible in small sacrifices, not only in grand gestures.

Chemistry cannot parent a child at 3 a.m., negotiate a budget, or pray through grief. Let excitement cool enough to evaluate steadiness, honesty, and perseverance.

Lying about what you want out of marriage going in because you're afraid you'll lose the relationship if you are honest is one of the worst kinds of fraud you could ever commit. ~ Gary L. Thomas

Picture your future children: is this the adult you want shaping their conscience? If infatuation blinds you, borrow the eyes of a trusted mentor or relative willing to speak plainly.

Look beyond the present when choosing a partner. Weddings photograph joy; marriage archives endurance. The person who flirts well at dinner may panic at medical bills. The person who feels boring on dates may be gold during disaster. Test for the latter.

Thomas also warns against smuggling hidden agendas into engagement — agreeing to children, church, or career moves you do not want because you fear losing the relationship. That deception is cruelty with a ring on it.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Clearer criteria do not guarantee easy marriage; they prevent predictable disaster. Strong unions are constructed daily through humility, repair, and shared purpose — not discovered intact on the wedding day. Spark can appear quickly; deep unity often needs nine to fourteen years before two separate stories feel like one life.

Enter therefore with permanence in view. Exit strategies whispered in the engagement season become self-fulfilling later.

Both spouses must practice:
• Humility
• Forgiveness
• Prayer
• Healthy conflict resolution skills
• Fidelity
• Friendship

Early years include clumsy adjustments while identities shift from solo to team. Consistency matters more than perfection.

If you have taken the advice in this summary so far, you approach marriage with clearer questions than most couples bring to the altar. That alone will not remove conflict, but it removes the illusion that conflict means you failed to find a mythical perfect match.

Successful marriage is marked by intimacy and oneness; that is not something that happens overnight. It typically takes from nine to fourteen years of togetherness for two individuals to stop thinking of themselves as individuals and to start thinking of themselves as a couple. Enter with that horizon in mind.

If you want to be free to serve Jesus, there's no question—stay single. Marriage takes a lot of time. But if you want to become more like Jesus, I can't imagine any better thing to do than to get married. Being married forces you to face some character issues you'd never have to face otherwise. ~ Gary L. Thomas

Try this

Before making the final decision to marry your love interest, check if they are at least average on:
• Spirituality
• Humility
• Forgiveness
• Conflict resolution

Try to be objective with your grading!

Read it till the end?
